title: Dragonlight Router — Multi-Provider LLM Routing Engine
description: Intelligent model selection across 8 providers. Given a role (logical task type), the router consults a hot-reloadable role-to-model matrix, filters against live provider catalogs, scores candidates on budget headroom and recent health, interleaves across providers, and returns a ranked list of model IDs. Also implements the canonical MBR→CBR→LBR three-function cascade for engine-style dispatch consumers.
author: Korrigon @ Dragonlight International

inputs.name: role
inputs.constraints: non-empty, must exist in role matrix or returns empty list

inputs.name: exclude_providers
inputs.constraints: provider names to skip, None = no exclusions

inputs.name: dispatch_order
inputs.constraints: frozen dataclass with intent_category, context_tokens, etc.

outputs.name: ranked_model_ids
outputs.guarantees: sorted by composite score descending, interleaved to max_consecutive_same_provider, capped at top_n, contains only models present in live catalog or whose provider was not fetched

outputs.name: engine_response
outputs.guarantees: contains full dispatch outcome with cost and latency telemetry; was_fallback=True if primary failed and cascade advanced

outputs.name: dispatch_failure
outputs.guarantees: returned only when ALL backends in cascade are exhausted

operations.name: select_models
operations.description: Return ranked model IDs for a role. Factory's primary entry point. Reads role matrix, refreshes catalog if stale, filters by live catalog, scores by budget/health/rank, interleaves providers, returns top_n.
preconditions: role matrix file exists (empty dict if missing, returns [])
budget tracker initialized with provider configs
postconditions: result is sorted by composite score descending
no provider appears more than max_consecutive_same_provider times consecutively
result length <= top_n
models from fetched providers are present in live catalog
excluded providers do not appear in result
composite score = rank*0.6 + budget_score*0.25 + health_score*0.15
side_effects: triggers catalog refresh if stale (async HTTP calls to providers)
triggers role matrix hot-reload if file mtime changed

operations.name: record_request
operations.description: Record request outcome for budget and health tracking. Feeds the feedback loop that keeps scores accurate.
preconditions: provider name is valid (unknown providers tracked with default-unlimited budget)
postconditions: budget RPM window updated with current timestamp
budget RPD counter incremented
health tracker: success resets error count, failure increments it
health tracker: success updates EMA latency (alpha=0.2)
health tracker: failure with http_status=404 triggers model retirement
health tracker: 3+ consecutive errors trips circuit breaker (60s cooldown)
side_effects: modifies in-memory health state (error counts, circuit breaker state)

operations.name: estimate_complexity
operations.description: Map a DispatchOrder to a required backend tier using heuristic signals. This is the MBR (Model Based Router) input — it determines the minimum capability tier.
postconditions: returns ComplexityEstimate with tier in {LOCAL, HAIKU, SONNET, OPUS}
OPUS intents: session_lifecycle, strategic_planning, complex_reasoning
SONNET intents: engineering_build, code_review, architecture, debugging, spec_writing
tool use requires minimum SONNET
large context (>=8000 tokens) requires minimum SONNET
short message + low context stays LOCAL
confidence is 0.7-0.9 depending on signal strength
signals list explains the reasoning chain

operations.name: interleave_providers
operations.description: Reorder scored models so no provider appears max_consecutive+1 times in a row. Preserves score ordering where possible.
preconditions: scored_models is sorted by composite score descending
postconditions: no provider appears more than max_consecutive times consecutively (unless only one provider exists)
relative score ordering preserved where constraint allows
total model count unchanged

operations.name: dispatch
operations.description: Full cascade dispatch for engine consumers. Takes a DispatchOrder, runs MBR→CBR→LBR, executes dispatch via selected backend adapter, returns EngineResponse or DispatchFailure.
preconditions: dispatch_order is a valid frozen DispatchOrder
at least one backend is registered in BackendRegistry
circuit breaker is not open for all candidates
postconditions: returns EngineResponse on success with cost and latency telemetry
returns DispatchFailure only when ALL backends exhausted
fallback chain tracks attempted backends
budget and health state updated for the backend used
dispatch log and budget update are transactional

edge_cases.scenario: role not found in role matrix
edge_cases.narrative: the application asks for a role that hasn't been configured yet

edge_cases.scenario: all models for a role are in circuit-open state
edge_cases.expected_behavior: return empty list (circuit-open models score 0 and may still appear if no alternatives exist)
edge_cases.narrative: all providers are having a bad day simultaneously

edge_cases.scenario: catalog is stale and refresh fails
edge_cases.expected_behavior: proceed with last-known-good catalog; log warning; models from unfetched providers pass through unfiltered
edge_cases.narrative: network partition during catalog refresh — degrade gracefully

edge_cases.expected_behavior: interleaving returns models as-is (no constraint possible); all traffic goes to that provider
edge_cases.narrative: minimal deployment with a single provider

edge_cases.expected_behavior: all providers score budget=0; ranked lower but not excluded (composite score still has rank and health components); if dispatch path: returns BudgetExceededError
edge_cases.narrative: the operator has spent their daily budget

edge_cases.expected_behavior: HealthTracker retires the model immediately; model is excluded from future selections until reinstated or catalog refresh removes it
edge_cases.narrative: provider decommissions a model without warning

edge_cases.scenario: role matrix file deleted at runtime
edge_cases.expected_behavior: hot-reload reads missing file as empty dict; select_models returns empty list; no crash
edge_cases.narrative: someone accidentally deletes the matrix file

edge_cases.scenario: concurrent select_models calls while catalog is refreshing
edge_cases.expected_behavior: select_models uses last-known-good catalog; refresh runs in background; no blocking or deadlock

error_states.behavior: raise exception from config loader with path and error details; fail fast at boot
log warning, skip provider, continue with remaining providers; partial results returned

error_states.trigger: budget state file corrupt on disk
error_states.behavior: load_budget_state returns None; fresh start with zero counters
return DispatchFailure with attempted_backends list and per-backend error details
error_states.recovery: operator intervention required; circuit breakers will open for failing backends

error_states.trigger: circuit breaker opens for a backend
error_states.behavior: backend excluded from routing for 60s; after cooldown, HALF_OPEN allows 1 probe; success → CLOSED, failure → re-OPEN

invariants: select_models never raises an exception for valid input — it returns empty list instead
composite score is deterministic given same inputs (rank, budget_score, health_score)
no provider appears more than max_consecutive_same_provider times consecutively (unless single-provider)
circuit breaker cooldown is always respected — no request sent to circuit-open backend within cooldown window
PII never crosses the Core→Engine boundary (pre-redacted by CAL); router never sees unredacted content
dispatch log write and budget update are transactional — both succeed or both roll back
role matrix hot-reload is atomic — no partial reads
catalog cache writes are atomic (.tmp → rename pattern)
budget persistence writes are atomic (.tmp → rename pattern)
model retirement via 404 is immediate and irreversible until explicit reinstatement or catalog refresh
composition order MBR → CBR → LBR is load-bearing and not configurable

data.constraint: MBR→CBR→LBR three-function composition order is load-bearing and not configurable; cascade router is ALWAYS engaged
Core produces DispatchOrder with pre-redacted IR; Engine never sees unredacted PII; router consumes DispatchOrder + real-time backend state
All backends implement GenerativeBackend protocol; OpenAI-compatible wire format; provider-agnostic abstraction
Cascade router is always engaged, not bypassed when all backends online; routing is complexity-driven; Per Session 49 decision (locked)
mypy strict mode; no untyped defs; all public APIs fully typed
ruff lint: E, F, I, UP, B, C4, SIM; line-length 100; target py311
pytest with asyncio_mode=auto; coverage fail-under=80; cov-report=term-missing
PII never crosses Core→Engine boundary; context filtering by trust tier (DIAN CECHT); principle of least information
Per-backend circuit breaker pattern; 3 consecutive errors → 60s cooldown; separate from engine-level breaker
catalog TTL 24h; budget flush interval 5s; default top_n=12; max_consecutive_same_provider=2
v0.1 SELECTS models only — does NOT dispatch; dispatch/ and adapters/ packages are reserved stubs for future release

data.action: get the best available LLM for a task without managing 8 different provider SDKs
data.outcome: one ranked list of model IDs replaces eight provider-specific integrations

narrative_arc.setup: the developer has an LLM application that needs to call different providers based on task type
narrative_arc.rising_action: they keep adding if/else blocks for provider selection, rate limits, health checks, and budget tracking
narrative_arc.climax: they install dragonlight-router, call select_models('code_review'), and get a ranked list instantly
narrative_arc.resolution: they use their existing OpenAI SDK with the top model, call record_request() after, and the router handles the rest

five_whys.question: Why do you need a router?
five_whys.answer: because managing 8 provider SDKs manually is error-prone and unmaintainable

five_whys.question: Why is it unmaintainable?
five_whys.answer: because each provider has different rate limits, health states, and cost profiles that change independently

five_whys.question: Why does that matter?
five_whys.answer: because hardcoding provider logic means the application breaks when a provider goes down or changes pricing

five_whys.question: Why is provider failure so impactful?
five_whys.answer: because without circuit breakers and fallbacks, one failing provider takes down the entire application

five_whys.question: Why is that the root concern?
five_whys.answer: because the application's reliability should not depend on any single provider's availability — the router must degrade gracefully

data.root_motivation: the router must make multi-provider LLM usage as reliable as single-provider usage, with automatic fallback and graceful degradation

edge_cases_as_complications.complication: all providers for a role are circuit-open
edge_cases_as_complications.resolution: return empty list — application retries later or uses cached response
retire model immediately from active catalog; next selection skips it
proceed with last-known-good catalog; degrade gracefully

error_states_as_failure_endings.failure: all backends fail during dispatch cascade
error_states_as_failure_endings.ending: return DispatchFailure with attempted_backends and error details; operator can diagnose
error_states_as_failure_endings.recovery_path: circuit breakers open; next request routes around failing backends automatically

data.action: control LLM spending across providers while maintaining service quality
data.outcome: budget tracking and cost-aware scoring prevent overspending without manual intervention

narrative_arc.setup: the operator has daily and monthly budgets per provider and globally
narrative_arc.rising_action: they manually check spend dashboards and disable providers when budgets approach limits
narrative_arc.climax: the router's BudgetTracker automatically scores providers lower as budgets deplete, naturally shifting traffic to cheaper alternatives
narrative_arc.resolution: spending stays within budget automatically; the operator only intervenes when ALL budgets are exhausted (deterministic-only mode)

five_whys.question: Why do you need budget tracking in the router?
five_whys.answer: because LLM API costs are unpredictable and can spike suddenly

five_whys.question: Why are costs unpredictable?
five_whys.answer: because usage patterns vary by task complexity and provider pricing changes

five_whys.question: Why does that matter?
five_whys.answer: because a single expensive request to Opus when Groq would suffice wastes budget unnecessarily

five_whys.question: Why is that waste significant?
five_whys.answer: because the difference between Opus ($15/mtok) and Groq (free) is 3+ orders of magnitude per token

five_whys.question: Why is that the root concern?
five_whys.answer: because cost-optimal routing is the difference between a sustainable LLM operation and an unsustainable one

data.root_motivation: the router must make cost-optimal decisions automatically — no human can track 8 providers' budgets in real-time

edge_cases_as_complications.resolution: budget score drops to 0 for all; composite score still includes rank and health; dispatch path returns BudgetExceededError

edge_cases_as_complications.complication: budget resets at midnight while requests are in-flight
edge_cases_as_complications.resolution: daily counter reset is atomic; in-flight requests counted against new day

error_states_as_failure_endings.failure: budget state file corrupted on disk
error_states_as_failure_endings.ending: fresh start with zero counters; no crash; slight budget tracking inaccuracy until next flush
error_states_as_failure_endings.recovery_path: automatic — state rebuilds from request recording

data.action: route generative requests through the canonical MBR→CBR→LBR cascade
data.outcome: every generative request receives optimal provider selection based on capability, cost, and load

narrative_arc.setup: the engine receives a DispatchOrder from Core with classified intent and pre-redacted IR
narrative_arc.rising_action: MBR filters by capability tier, CBR scores by cost, LBR enforces rate limits and selects the best candidate
narrative_arc.climax: the cascade produces a DispatchDecision and executes dispatch via the PAL adapter
narrative_arc.resolution: an EngineResponse returns with cost telemetry, fallback tracking, and health feedback recorded

five_whys.question: Why is the cascade always engaged?
five_whys.answer: because even simple requests should use the cheapest adequate provider, not the most expensive default

five_whys.question: Why does that matter?
five_whys.answer: because 48% of interactions are classified as deterministic but currently run through the full generative pipeline

five_whys.question: Why is that waste significant?
five_whys.answer: because that represents an estimated $55,739 in avoidable Opus-equivalent cost annually

five_whys.question: Why hasn't this been fixed before?
five_whys.answer: because without the cascade router, there's no mechanism to route based on complexity

five_whys.question: Why is that the root concern?
five_whys.answer: because intelligent routing IS the product — the router exists to make every request cost-optimal by default

data.root_motivation: every request must be routed through the cascade — the router is not optional, it is the core of Engine's intelligence

edge_cases_as_complications.complication: no candidates match the required tier
edge_cases_as_complications.resolution: adjacent-tier graceful upgrade: load candidates from one tier higher; NEVER downgrade
scoring weights shift to favor cost heavily (cost weight 0.70); next request routes to cheapest adequate provider

error_states_as_failure_endings.ending: DispatchFailure returned with full diagnostic; operator sees which backends failed and why
error_states_as_failure_endings.recovery_path: circuit breakers open for failing backends; next request skips them automatically

data.title: implement MBR (Model Based Router) — capability filtering stage
data.description: The first stage of the three-function cascade. Takes DispatchOrder + BackendRegistry, filters candidates by complexity tier match + health. Implements adjacent-tier graceful upgrade (one tier higher if zero candidates). Outputs candidate set for CBR.

acceptance_criteria.criterion: MBR filters candidates to those matching DispatchOrder.complexity_tier OR one tier above
MBR excludes backends with circuit_open status
MBR performs graceful upgrade to next tier when zero candidates in requested tier
MBR NEVER downgrades — complex request never routed to medium/haiku tier
MBR includes local providers as unlimited-rate in LBR passthrough

data.notes: New file — MBR logic does not exist yet as a discrete stage.

data.title: implement CBR (Cost Balancing Router) — cost scoring stage
data.description: The second stage. Takes MBR candidate set + budget tracker + cost governor, filters budget-exhausted providers, scores by projected cost with budget-pressure weight shifting. Implements CostGovernor activation logic.

acceptance_criteria.criterion: CBR excludes providers with spent_usd >= budget_usd (hard filter)
CBR scores candidates using ScoringWeights (cost=0.35, latency=0.25, priority=0.20, queue=0.10, health=0.10)
Cost governor activates when daily/monthly spend exceeds cost_down_threshold
When cost governor active, weights shift to cost=0.70, latency=0.10, priority=0.10, queue=0.05, health=0.05
When ALL providers exceed budget, returns BudgetExceededError

data.notes: New file — canonical ScoringWeights and CostGovernor not yet implemented.

data.title: implement LBR (Load Balancing Router) — rate enforcement + final selection
data.description: The third stage. Takes CBR-scored candidates + rate limit tracker, filters rate-saturated providers, enforces rolling windows, breaks ties, implements privacy rotation for untrusted tier. Outputs final DispatchDecision.

acceptance_criteria.criterion: LBR excludes providers where projected request count would exceed RPM/RPD limits
LBR deprioritizes providers within 80% of rate limits (queue policy mode)
LBR selects top candidate and returns DispatchDecision
LBR treats local providers as unlimited-rate (hardware constraints only)
Zero candidates remaining after LBR → RoutingError with diagnostic info

data.notes: New file. Current interleave.py handles provider spreading but not rate-limit enforcement or privacy rotation.

data.title: implement cascade dispatch (MBR→CBR→LBR composition + execution)
data.description: Wire the three stages together as the canonical route() function. Takes DispatchOrder, runs MBR→CBR→LBR, dispatches via selected backend adapter, returns EngineResponse or DispatchFailure. Implements fallback cascade with transactional dispatch log + budget update.

acceptance_criteria.criterion: route() applies MBR→CBR→LBR in fixed order (not configurable)
On primary backend failure, cascade advances to next candidate (fallback)
EngineResponse.was_fallback=True when cascade advanced
EngineResponse.fallback_chain lists all attempted backends
Dispatch log write + budget update are transactional (both or neither)
All backends exhausted → DispatchFailure (not exception)

data.notes: The dispatch/ package is currently an empty stub. This is the biggest missing feature.

data.title: implement GenerativeBackend adapters for all 8 providers
data.description: Create concrete implementations of the GenerativeBackend protocol for: NVIDIA NIM, Groq, OpenRouter, Cerebras, Gemini, Mistral, Anthropic, Ollama. Each adapter handles provider-specific auth headers, endpoint paths, streaming format, and health checks.

acceptance_criteria.criterion: Each adapter implements generate(), health_check(), record_usage() per GenerativeBackend protocol
All adapters use OpenAI-compatible wire format (chat completions)
Anthropic adapter handles non-standard /v1/messages endpoint
Ollama adapter requires no API key
Each adapter reads its API key from the configured env_key

data.notes: The adapters/ package is currently an empty stub.

data.title: implement context trust tier filtering (DIAN CECHT)
data.description: Implement filter_context_for_provider() that strips system-level context based on provider trust tier. TIER 1 (TRUSTED): pass through. TIER 2 (SEMI_TRUSTED): omit behavioral rules, replace persona names, limit history. TIER 3 (UNTRUSTED): task instruction only. TIER 3-LOCAL: full context (no network egress).

acceptance_criteria.criterion: TRUSTED providers receive full system-level context (minus PII, already absent)
SEMI_TRUSTED providers receive context without behavioral rules or persona names
UNTRUSTED providers receive task-specific instruction only
LOCAL providers receive full context (no network egress risk)
PII is never present regardless of tier (pre-redacted by CAL)

data.notes: New file. Security-critical module — tests must verify no information leakage.

data.title: implement cost governor + ScoringWeights per canonical spec
data.description: Replace the current simple compute_composite_score (rank=0.6, budget=0.25, health=0.15) with the canonical ScoringWeights (cost=0.35, latency=0.25, priority=0.20, queue=0.10, health=0.10). Implement CostGovernorConfig, cost_governor_active(), cost_adjusted_weights().

acceptance_criteria.criterion: ScoringWeights dataclass with canonical default values
score_candidate() normalizes each dimension to [0.0, 1.0]
cost_governor_active() returns True when daily or monthly spend exceeds threshold
cost_adjusted_weights() shifts cost weight to 0.70 when governor active
score is deterministic given same inputs
score >= 0.0 always

data.notes: Refactor existing scoring.py — current simple weights remain for select_models path; canonical weights for dispatch path.

data.title: implement health check background loop
data.description: Background asyncio task that runs every 30s, checking each enabled provider's health via health_check(). Updates BackendState status. Implements SLO enforcement: providers exceeding latency SLO for 3 consecutive checks transition to degraded.

acceptance_criteria.criterion: health check loop runs every 30 seconds for all enabled providers
providers exceeding latency SLO for 3 consecutive checks transition to degraded
degraded providers receive ranking penalty but are not excluded
health check failures do not crash the loop

data.notes: New file. Currently health tracking is passive (only updated on record_request).

data.title: enhance HTTP API with dispatch endpoint and trust-tier headers
data.description: Add POST /v1/dispatch endpoint for engine-style consumers. Add trust-tier and complexity-tier information to /v1/select and /v1/health responses. Add POST /v1/retire and POST /v1/reinstate endpoints for manual model lifecycle management.

acceptance_criteria.criterion: POST /v1/dispatch accepts DispatchOrder JSON and returns EngineResponse or DispatchFailure
/v1/select response includes trust_tier and complexity_tier per model
POST /v1/retire and POST /v1/reinstate manage model lifecycle
All endpoints return structured error responses (never raw exceptions)

data.title: wire RouterEngine.dispatch() method for engine consumers
data.description: Add dispatch() method to RouterEngine that takes a DispatchOrder and runs the full cascade. Wire it into the HTTP API. Preserve select_models() backward compatibility.

acceptance_criteria.criterion: RouterEngine.dispatch(DispatchOrder) returns EngineResponse | DispatchFailure
select_models() behavior unchanged (backward compatible)
dispatch() integrates MBR→CBR→LBR + PAL adapter + fallback cascade
no ImportError when loading modified RouterEngine

data.title: implement integration test suite for end-to-end cascade routing
data.description: Integration tests that exercise the full MBR→CBR→LBR→dispatch→response path with mock backends. Verify fallback cascade, circuit breaker integration, budget exhaustion, and graceful degradation scenarios.

acceptance_criteria.criterion: integration test: full cascade with all healthy backends selects optimal provider
integration test: primary failure triggers fallback to next candidate
integration test: all backends failing returns DispatchFailure
integration test: circuit breaker opens after 3 consecutive errors
integration test: budget exhaustion deprioritizes expensive providers
integration test: catalog refresh failure degrades gracefully

data.notes: New integration test file. Uses mock backends (no real API calls).

data.title: improve BudgetTracker with TPM and daily token cap enforcement
data.description: Extend BudgetTracker to track TPM (tokens per minute) and daily token cap. Add token-based sliding window for TPM. Wire into LBR rate-limit filtering.

acceptance_criteria.criterion: BudgetTracker tracks TPM via sliding window (same as RPM)
BudgetTracker enforces daily token cap (0 = unlimited)
score() incorporates TPM headroom into budget score
has_capacity() returns False when TPM or token cap exhausted

data.notes: Extends existing BudgetTracker. TPM and daily_token_cap fields exist in types but aren't tracked.

sessions.summary: Session 49 decision (locked): cascade router is ALWAYS engaged, not bypassed when all backends online.
Phase 1 audit: cascade router implementation is production-ready at bridge/cascade.py (~1K lines) with 75+ tests.
open_questions_remaining: context trust tier filtering not implemented

sessions.summary: CAL-PAL architecture exploration: cascade router sits in Engine between Core's CAL output and PAL adapter selection.
topics_covered: OS analogues for cascade router (MBR=capsched, CBR=cgroups, LBR=WFQ)

research_notes.findings: 48% of interactions classified as deterministic but run through full generative pipeline. Working cascade router would eliminate estimated $55,739 in avoidable Opus-equivalent cost.
research_notes.decision: Three-tier cascade: LOCAL/HAIKU/SONNET/OPUS with complexity-driven routing

research_notes.findings: RPM limits vary 100x across providers (2rpm Mistral to 30rpm Groq). Daily caps vary similarly.
research_notes.decision: LBR must track RPM, RPD, TPM, and daily token cap per provider

research_notes.topic: v0.1 architectural decision — select only, no dispatch
research_notes.findings: Separating selection from dispatch allows any consumer to use the router with their own HTTP client.
research_notes.decision: v0.1 returns ranked list; v0.2 adds dispatch path as separate capability

data.ideal_state: every LLM request is routed to the cheapest adequate provider automatically, with circuit breakers preventing cascading failures, budget tracking preventing overspend, and graceful degradation when providers fail

chain: because managing multiple LLM providers manually is unsustainable
because each provider has different rate limits, costs, and health states
because without intelligent routing, applications overpay or break
because the cost difference between providers is 3+ orders of magnitude
because cost-optimal routing is the difference between sustainable and unsustainable LLM operations
five_whys_traces.root_motivation: the router must make every request cost-optimal by default while maintaining reliability across provider failures

formalization_sessions.summary: Created live-spec from reference corpus analysis

decisions_made.question: should the live-spec cover both select_models and dispatch paths?
decisions_made.decision: yes — select_models is v0.1 (existing), dispatch is v0.2 (to build)
decisions_made.rationale: the live-spec must capture the full intended capability

decisions_made.question: should canonical ScoringWeights replace the current simple weights?
decisions_made.decision: both coexist — simple weights for select_models, canonical weights for dispatch path

decisions_made.question: should the context trust tier filtering be in this spec?
decisions_made.decision: yes — it's security-critical and referenced in canonical spec Section 8.7
decisions_made.rationale: DIAN CECHT is a load-bearing security pattern

gaps.note: AsyncIterator[str] streaming dispatch path needs spec detail
referenced in canonical spec but is a separate spec concern

gaps.area: privacy rotation algorithm for LBR untrusted tier
gaps.note: mentioned in canonical spec but details deferred to implementation

data.name: dragonlight_router.router
data.description: Top-level orchestrator. Holds all subsystems (BudgetTracker, HealthTracker, RoleMatrix, CatalogCache, ScoringEngine). select_models() is the factory entry point. dispatch() is the engine entry point (v0.2).

data.name: dragonlight_router.selection.scoring
data.description: Composite scoring engine. compute_composite_score (simple weights) for select_models path. ScoringWeights + score_candidate + CostGovernor (canonical weights) for dispatch path.
data.status: existing — needs canonical ScoringWeights and CostGovernor (TM-007)

data.name: dragonlight_router.selection.interleave
data.description: Provider interleaving — prevents max_consecutive_same_provider runs. Used by select_models().

data.name: dragonlight_router.selection.complexity
data.description: Complexity tier heuristic — maps DispatchOrder to BackendTier. Implements tool-use and long-context signals. Used by MBR.

data.name: dragonlight_router.selection.mbr
data.description: Model Based Router — first stage of cascade. Filters by tier + health. Implements graceful upgrade.

data.name: dragonlight_router.selection.cbr
data.description: Cost Balancing Router — second stage. Filters budget-exhausted, scores by cost with governor logic.

data.name: dragonlight_router.selection.lbr
data.description: Load Balancing Router — third stage. Enforces rate limits, breaks ties, implements privacy rotation.

data.name: dragonlight_router.selection.context_filter
data.description: Context trust tier filtering (DIAN CECHT). Strips system-level context based on provider trust tier before dispatch.

data.name: dragonlight_router.dispatch.cascade
data.description: Cascade dispatch — wires MBR→CBR→LBR composition, executes dispatch via PAL adapter, returns EngineResponse or DispatchFailure. Implements fallback cascade.

data.name: dragonlight_router.health.tracker
data.description: Per-model health scoring and availability tracking. Uses CircuitBreaker for each model. Handles 404 retirement.

data.name: dragonlight_router.health.circuit_breaker
data.description: Per-model circuit breaker. CLOSED→OPEN→HALF_OPEN state machine. 3 errors in 120s → 60s cooldown.

data.name: dragonlight_router.budget.tracker
data.description: Per-provider budget tracking with RPM/RPD sliding windows. score() returns 0-100 budget availability. Persists state to disk.
data.status: existing — needs TPM and daily token cap (TM-012)

data.name: dragonlight_router.catalog.cache
data.description: File-backed catalog cache with TTL. Atomic writes via .tmp→rename. Stale cache returns None.

data.name: dragonlight_router.catalog.refresher
data.description: Async concurrent catalog fetcher. Calls GET /v1/models on each provider. Returns partial results (failed providers skipped).

data.name: dragonlight_router.core.types
data.description: Frozen data types for the entire routing system. All configuration and request/response types. GenerativeBackend protocol definition.

data.name: dragonlight_router.core.registry
data.description: Backend registry — constructed once at boot, queried per-request. Maps name → (GenerativeBackend, BackendState).

data.name: dragonlight_router.core.state
data.description: Mutable runtime state per backend. Tracks RPM/RPD capacity, errors, circuit breaker, latency EMA. NASA Power-of-10 invariant checks.

data.name: dragonlight_router.config.schema
data.description: Pydantic configuration schema. Top-level RouterConfig loaded from YAML.

data.name: dragonlight_router.config.loader
data.description: Configuration loader. Resolution: explicit path → DRAGONLIGHT_ROUTER_CONFIG env → defaults.

data.name: dragonlight_router.cache.simple
data.description: SHA-256 keyed response cache with TTL. Deterministic key from (model_id, system_prompt, messages, temperature, max_tokens).

data.name: dragonlight_router.cache.semantic
data.description: Similarity-based response cache using TF-IDF + cosine similarity. Threshold configurable.

data.name: dragonlight_router.server.app
data.description: FastAPI application factory. Creates app with CORS, routes, and lifespan handler.

data.name: dragonlight_router.server.routes
data.description: HTTP route handlers. GET /v1/select, GET /v1/health, POST /v1/record. Needs POST /v1/dispatch, POST /v1/retire, POST /v1/reinstate.

data.name: dragonlight_router.role_matrix
data.description: Hot-reloadable YAML role-to-model mapping. Watches file mtime. Atomic loads. Returns ranked model list per role.

data.method_signature: async def dispatch(self, order: DispatchOrder) -> EngineResponse | DispatchFailure
data.integration_notes: Add dispatch() method to RouterEngine. Import dispatch.cascade.route. Wire into HTTP API via server/routes.py POST /v1/dispatch. No changes to select_models() — fully backward compatible.
new_imports: from dragonlight_router.core.types import DispatchOrder, EngineResponse, DispatchFailure
data.failure_mode_if_wrong: ImportError at module load time if dispatch.cascade not available; dispatch() must handle case where no backends registered

data.integration_notes: Add canonical ScoringWeights dataclass and score_candidate() alongside existing compute_composite_score(). Both coexist — simple weights for select_models, canonical for dispatch path.
data.failure_mode_if_wrong: score_candidate() normalization bug would produce wrong routing decisions silently — must have exhaustive normalization tests

data.integration_notes: Add new FastAPI routes to existing register_routes() function. Dispatch endpoint is async. Retire/reinstate are sync. All return structured JSON responses.
data.failure_mode_if_wrong: Malformed request body returns 422 with structured error; dispatch failure returns 503 with DispatchFailure JSON

data.method_signature: def has_tpm_capacity(self, provider_name: str) -> bool; def record_tokens(self, provider_name: str, tokens: int) -> None
data.integration_notes: Add TPM sliding window tracking (same deque pattern as RPM). Add daily token cap tracking. Wire into score() and has_capacity().
data.failure_mode_if_wrong: TPM tracking misconfiguration could over-restrict or under-restrict provider usage; token cap 0 must mean unlimited

calls.reason: hot-reload role matrix if file changed
get candidate models for role with ranks
check if cached catalog is fresh (within TTL)
refresh stale catalogs concurrently
get budget availability score per provider
get health score per model
calculate weighted composite score
prevent consecutive same-provider runs
return value

calls.to: route(order, registry, budget_tracker, rate_tracker, health_cache, cost_governor, queue_depths, config)
calls.reason: run MBR→CBR→LBR cascade
MBR: classify dispatch order into BackendTier
MBR: filter by tier match + health
CBR: hard-filter budget-exhausted
CBR: score by cost with governor
CBR: check if cost governor should override weights
CBR: shift weights when governor active
LBR: hard-filter rate-saturated providers
LBR: pick top candidate, break ties
return value
strip system-level context by trust tier before dispatch
execute dispatch via selected backend adapter
record token consumption for budget tracking
record request for rate limit tracking
record success and latency for health tracking
record error for health/circuit tracking
fallback to next candidate on failure
return value
track RPM window + increment RPD counter
track token consumption (if TPM/daily cap enabled)
reset error count, update EMA latency
increment error count, may trip circuit or retire model
may trip circuit breaker if threshold reached
immediate retirement on 404
reset circuit to CLOSED, clear error timestamps
fetch catalog from single provider
GET /v1/models from provider endpoint
persist fetched catalog to disk with atomic write
